name: Office of the First Consul of the French Republic
constraints: Nominal constitutional framework of the Consulate requires Council of State deliberation for major legislative acts, though Napoleon routinely circumvents its spirit
Military campaigns and colonial ventures are constrained by treasury insolvency and the ruinous cost of the Saint-Domingue expedition
The Peace of Amiens with Britain is fragile; any action perceived as aggressive expansion risks renewed war before France is prepared
Legitimacy rests on plebiscitary consent and military prestige, both of which require continuous success—failure in the Caribbean erodes both
factional_pressures: Idealist imperialists within Napoleon's inner circle, led by his brothers Joseph and Lucien, who oppose the sale of Louisiana as a betrayal of French grandeur
Fiscal realists centred on Barbe-Marbois at the Treasury, who regard the sale as the only means of funding renewed European war
Talleyrand's Foreign Ministry, which pursues its own diplomatic choreography and is not above withholding or shaping intelligence to serve Talleyrand's private interests
Royalist and Jacobin remnants who watch the Consulate for any sign of weakness that might reopen the question of France's political future
public_legitimacy: Exceptionally high among the French populace, who associate Napoleon with order, military glory, and the consolidation of revolutionary gains after a decade of chaos; the Consulate is perceived as the republic stabilised rather than betrayed, though this legitimacy is contingent on continued victory.
plausible_actions: Authorise the secret sale of Louisiana to the United States through direct negotiation, bypassing formal treaty convention
Dispatch a replacement expeditionary force to Saint-Domingue or, conversely, abandon the island to its fate and redirect resources to Europe
Instruct Talleyrand to conduct deliberate diplomatic delay with American envoys while Napoleon reaches a final decision
Elevate the Consulate to a hereditary empire by plebiscite, consolidating personal power under constitutional cover
Renew hostilities with Britain upon the collapse of the Peace of Amiens, fundamentally altering the strategic calculus around Louisiana
implausible_actions: Submit the Louisiana sale to a genuinely deliberative legislative vote that Napoleon does not control or predetermine
Voluntarily cede Saint-Domingue's sovereignty to Toussaint Louverture or his successors through formal treaty recognising Haitian independence
Pursue a permanent, good-faith alliance with the United States as an equal partner rather than as a temporary expedient

name: United States Executive Branch under Thomas Jefferson
constraints: The Constitution contains no explicit provision authorising the federal government to acquire foreign territory, forcing Jefferson to act against his own strict-constructionist doctrine
The Senate must ratify any treaty by a two-thirds majority, and the House must appropriate the purchase funds, making congressional management indispensable

The foreign service is skeletal—a minister in Paris, a minister in London, and a handful of consuls—making real-time coordination across the Atlantic nearly impossible given mail delays of six to eight weeks
Jefferson's Republican coalition includes Southern planters deeply suspicious of federal power and Western settlers whose demands may outrun constitutional propriety
factional_pressures: Old Republicans led by John Randolph of Roanoke, who regard any expansion of federal executive authority as a betrayal of the revolution of 1800 and will scrutinise the purchase for constitutional violations

Federalists under Hamilton and Pickering, who privately support the acquisition but will oppose it publicly if it can be used to embarrass Jefferson or argue for a stronger federal government than he has admitted
Western settlers and their congressional representatives, who demand immediate resolution of the New Orleans deposit crisis and will not wait upon diplomatic niceties
New England commercial interests who fear that the addition of vast new agricultural territory will permanently shift political power southward and westward, diluting their influence
public_legitimacy: High among the agrarian majority and Western settlers who form Jefferson's electoral base; contested among New England Federalists who regard the Republican administration as ideologically reckless and constitutionally opportunistic.
plausible_actions: Dispatch special envoys to Paris with authority to purchase New Orleans and the Floridas, and—if fortune permits—the whole of Louisiana
Threaten a military alliance with Britain to pressure France into negotiation, as Jefferson's letter to Robert Livingston in April 1802 actually contemplated
Ratify the Louisiana Purchase treaty under the implied powers of the Constitution while privately acknowledging the constitutional difficulty
Organise the governance of the acquired territory through enabling legislation, creating territorial governments subordinate to federal authority
Suppress public debate about the constitutional irregularity of the purchase by moving with deliberate speed before opposition can organise
implausible_actions: Seek a constitutional amendment before completing the purchase, as Jefferson briefly contemplated—the diplomatic window would close before ratification could be achieved
Refer the question of territorial acquisition to the Supreme Court for an advisory opinion prior to action
Offer to purchase Louisiana jointly with Britain, sharing sovereignty over the territory

name: United States Senate
constraints: Treaty ratification requires a two-thirds supermajority, meaning a determined minority can defeat any agreement regardless of popular sentiment

Senators serve six-year terms and are elected by state legislatures, not directly by the people, insulating them from immediate popular pressure but making them responsive to state-level factional interests
The Senate's constitutional role gives it a check on executive foreign policy, but no mechanism to initiate negotiation or compel diplomatic action
The speed of the purchase negotiations—concluded in weeks—gives opponents little time to organise, but the constitutional silence on territorial acquisition gives them potent ammunition
factional_pressures: Jeffersonian Republicans hold a majority but are divided between pragmatists willing to overlook constitutional ambiguity and doctrinaire Old Republicans who regard the purchase as executive usurpation
Federalist senators, a shrinking minority, are ideologically tempted to support the purchase but politically incentivised to oppose anything that enhances Jefferson's prestige
New England senators fear the demographic and electoral consequences of admitting a vast new territory likely to produce Republican states
Western-state senators and those representing Mississippi Territory constituencies exert intense pressure for rapid ratification, viewing delay as an existential threat to frontier livelihoods
public_legitimacy: Regarded by Republicans as a necessary check on executive power; regarded by Federalists as the last institutional bulwark against Jeffersonian majoritarianism; largely opaque to ordinary citizens, whose political engagement is mediated through newspapers and local party organisations.
plausible_actions: Ratify the Louisiana Purchase treaty with a comfortable majority, despite private constitutional misgivings among Republican members
Attach conditions or reservations to ratification demanding clarification of the territory's legal status and the rights of its inhabitants
Appropriate the fifteen million dollars required for the purchase through a combination of stock issuance and European banking arrangements
Hold extended debate that serves as a public airing of constitutional anxieties without ultimately blocking the treaty
Investigate the conduct of American envoys in Paris to determine whether they exceeded their instructions
implausible_actions: Reject the Louisiana Purchase treaty outright on constitutional grounds, given the overwhelming political momentum and Western settler pressure
Initiate its own independent diplomatic channel to France, bypassing the executive branch entirely
Demand that the acquired territory be immediately admitted as a full state rather than governed as a territory

name: French Ministry of Foreign Affairs under Talleyrand
constraints: Talleyrand's authority is entirely derivative of Napoleon's confidence, which is itself conditional on results; he operates as an instrument of the First Consul's will, not as an independent policy-maker
The ministry's correspondence with French agents in America and the Caribbean is subject to British naval interception, compromising operational security
Talleyrand's known venality—his habit of soliciting payments from foreign governments—has permanently damaged his credibility with American diplomats since the XYZ Affair of 1797-98

Napoleon's practice of conducting decisive negotiations through private audiences and trusted intermediaries rather than formal diplomatic channels reduces Talleyrand to a stage manager rather than a principal
factional_pressures: Napoleon's brothers, particularly Joseph and Lucien Bonaparte, who regard Talleyrand as an untrustworthy survivor of too many regimes and compete for influence over foreign policy
American envoys Livingston and Monroe, who have learned from the XYZ experience to distrust Talleyrand's overtures and seek direct access to Napoleon or Barbe-Marbois
Royalist sympathisers within the ministry's permanent staff who regard Talleyrand's service to the Consulate as merely the latest in a series of convenient loyalties
Spanish diplomatic pressure regarding the terms of the retrocession of Louisiana, which France has not fully honoured, creating legal complications Talleyrand must manage or suppress
public_legitimacy: Negligible as a public institution; its legitimacy derives entirely from Napoleon's authority. Talleyrand himself is regarded by informed observers as a man of extraordinary intelligence and zero principle—an assessment he does not trouble to refute.
plausible_actions: Conduct deliberate delay in negotiations with Livingston, keeping the Americans in uncertainty while Napoleon reaches his decision on Saint-Domingue and the European situation
Arrange for Barbe-Marbois to conduct the decisive negotiation with Monroe and Livingston, effectively sidelining the Foreign Ministry at the crucial moment
Communicate false or misleading intelligence to Napoleon about American intentions to shape the First Consul's decision in directions favourable to Talleyrand's private interests
Manage Spanish protests about the illegality of the sale—Spain had been promised Louisiana would never be transferred to a third power—through diplomatic evasion and delay
Position himself to profit financially from the transaction through intermediaries, as was his established practice
implausible_actions: Openly oppose Napoleon's decision to sell Louisiana once the First Consul has resolved upon it
Conduct transparent, good-faith negotiations with American envoys without pursuing a parallel private agenda
Resign his ministry on grounds of principle regarding the surrender of French imperial territory

name: French Ministry of the Treasury under Barbe-Marbois
constraints: The Treasury's influence depends entirely on the severity of France's fiscal crisis; in periods of military success and revenue, its counsel is less urgently heeded
Barbe-Marbois must work within Napoleon's ultimate authority and cannot compel a sale that the First Consul has not decided upon

Any financial arrangement with the United States must be structured through European banking houses—specifically Hope and Company of Amsterdam and Baring Brothers of London—whose cooperation is not guaranteed
The terms of the purchase must be negotiated rapidly, before the diplomatic window closes with the resumption of Anglo-French hostilities
factional_pressures: Napoleon's imperial faction, which regards the sale as a humiliation and argues that France should retain Louisiana regardless of the fiscal cost

European banking interests, particularly the Barings and Hope partnerships, who will profit from the transaction but whose involvement requires delicate management given that Britain is a likely future enemy
American negotiators who, once offered the whole of Louisiana, will press for the lowest possible price and the most favourable payment terms
French military creditors and contractors whose demands on the treasury make rapid infusion of American funds a matter of operational urgency
public_legitimacy: The Treasury operates as a technical institution without popular constituency; its legitimacy is bureaucratic rather than democratic. Barbe-Marbois's personal reputation for probity—unusual in an era of spectacular venality—lends the ministry a credibility that Talleyrand's Foreign Ministry conspicuously lacks.
plausible_actions: Present Napoleon with a detailed fiscal analysis demonstrating that the cost of defending Louisiana against British naval power exceeds any conceivable revenue from the territory
Conduct direct, confidential negotiations with Monroe and Livingston, bypassing Talleyrand's ministry at Napoleon's implicit authorisation
Structure the purchase price as a combination of American government bonds and assumed American claims against France, reducing the immediate cash burden on the United States
Arrange the financial mechanics of the transfer through the Baring-Hope banking consortium, ensuring that funds reach the French treasury regardless of Anglo-French diplomatic relations
Advise Napoleon on the minimum acceptable price—sixty million francs for the territory plus twenty million in assumed claims—and hold that floor in negotiation
implausible_actions: Refuse to participate in the sale on the grounds that Louisiana is a strategic asset whose value exceeds any monetary consideration
Conduct the financial arrangements through the French state banking system alone, without recourse to private European banking houses
Propose that France retain a financial stake or reversionary interest in Louisiana as a condition of the sale

name: Spanish Colonial Administration of Louisiana
constraints: The Treaty of San Ildefonso in 1800 has secretly retroceded Louisiana to France, but formal transfer has not occurred, leaving Spanish officials nominally in authority over a territory that is legally no longer theirs
The Intendant and Governor-General in New Orleans receive instructions from Madrid that are months out of date and increasingly irrelevant to rapidly changing circumstances
Spanish military forces in the territory are insufficient to resist either French or American pressure; the garrison at New Orleans is a symbol rather than a strategic asset

The suspension of the American right of deposit in 1802 was ordered by the Spanish Intendant without Madrid's explicit authorisation, creating a diplomatic crisis that the administration cannot resolve on its own authority
factional_pressures: Spanish Creole elites who have prospered under colonial administration and fear the consequences of transfer to either France or the United States for their property rights and social precedence
American merchants in New Orleans who regard Spanish authority as an obstacle to commerce and agitate openly for American acquisition of the city
French Creole society, which maintains cultural and emotional ties to France while having accommodated itself to Spanish governance over three decades
Madrid's shifting diplomatic alignments—Spain is simultaneously a French ally and a power with its own imperial interests in the Americas—creating contradictory instructions to colonial officials
public_legitimacy: Declining rapidly; accepted by Creole elites as a familiar and relatively tolerant overlord, but regarded with impatience by American commercial interests and with conditional loyalty by French Creoles who anticipate French reacquisition. The suspension of the deposit right has fatally damaged Spanish legitimacy among the American settler population of the trans-Appalachian West.
plausible_actions: Restore the American right of deposit under pressure from Madrid, which fears that its suspension will provoke an American military response Spain cannot repel
Delay the formal transfer of Louisiana to France on procedural grounds, hoping that changed circumstances—renewed Anglo-French war, Napoleon's death—might reverse the retrocession
Negotiate quietly with American agents about the status of the Floridas, which Spain retains and which Jefferson covets as much as Louisiana itself
Attempt to extract concessions from France regarding the terms of the retrocession, including guarantees of Creole property rights and Spanish trading privileges
Maintain public order in New Orleans through a combination of patronage to Creole elites and suppression of American agitation
implausible_actions: Formally ally with the United States against France to prevent the retrocession of Louisiana
Unilaterally declare Louisiana an independent state rather than submit to French reacquisition
Mobilise sufficient military force to resist a French or American occupation of New Orleans

name: Roman Catholic Church in Louisiana (Diocese of Louisiana and the Floridas)
constraints: The diocese is suffragant to the Bishop of Santiago de Cuba, whose authority derives from Spanish royal patronage; a change of sovereignty threatens the entire institutional structure of ecclesiastical appointment and funding

The Church's property holdings and legal privileges in Louisiana are secured under Spanish colonial law, which may not survive transfer to a French republic that has only recently and incompletely reconciled with Rome through the Concordat of 1801
The clergy in Louisiana are predominantly French Creole in sympathy, creating tension with a Spanish episcopal hierarchy that has governed them for three decades

American Protestant immigration into the territory, certain to accelerate after any transfer, poses a long-term threat to Catholic cultural dominance that the Church cannot address through institutional means alone
factional_pressures: French Creole parishioners who regard the Church as the primary institution of their cultural identity and demand that it protect their interests through any political transition
Spanish clergy appointed under royal patronage who fear losing their positions and income if Spanish ecclesiastical jurisdiction is replaced by French or American arrangements
Bishop John Carroll of Baltimore, who has long sought to extend American Catholic episcopal authority over Louisiana and sees the Purchase as an opportunity
The Vatican's own diplomatic calculations regarding the Concordat with Napoleon, which has restored some Church privileges in France but on terms that subordinate ecclesiastical to state authority
public_legitimacy: The highest of any institution in Louisiana among the French Creole population; the Church anchors social life, education, charitable provision, and cultural identity in ways that no civil institution can replicate. Its legitimacy among American Protestant settlers is negligible, and its relationship with the enslaved African population is complex—simultaneously a vehicle of spiritual consolation and an instrument of social control.
plausible_actions: Petition the incoming American administration for guarantees of Catholic property rights and freedom of worship as a condition of Creole cooperation with the transfer
Negotiate with Bishop Carroll of Baltimore regarding the future episcopal jurisdiction of Louisiana, seeking terms that preserve French Creole clerical appointments
Use the pulpit and confessional to shape Creole public opinion regarding the political transition, either counselling accommodation or resistance depending on what terms are secured
Appeal to the Holy See for intervention with Napoleon to protect Church interests in any treaty of cession
Maintain the Church's role in recording births, marriages, and deaths—and thus in adjudicating property inheritance—as a source of institutional power that transcends political sovereignty
implausible_actions: Formally excommunicate American Protestant settlers as a means of resisting the cultural consequences of the Purchase
Organise armed resistance to the transfer of Louisiana on behalf of Spanish colonial authority
Voluntarily surrender its property holdings and legal privileges in advance of the transfer as a gesture of accommodation to the incoming American administration

name: Cabildo of New Orleans
constraints: The Cabildo's authority derives from Spanish colonial law and royal charter; its legal basis evaporates upon transfer of sovereignty, leaving its members uncertain whether their offices, ordinances, and judicial decisions will be recognised by any successor government
The body is composed almost entirely of French Creole elites appointed under Spanish patronage, creating a structural tension between their cultural identity and their institutional loyalty
Its jurisdiction extends only to the city of New Orleans and its immediate environs; it has no authority over the vast interior of the territory whose fate is being decided in Paris and Washington
Its financial resources are dependent on Spanish colonial appropriations that will cease upon transfer, leaving it unable to fund basic municipal functions during any interregnum
factional_pressures: Creole merchant families who dominate the Cabildo and whose commercial interests require stable property law, reliable contract enforcement, and access to both the Mississippi trade and Atlantic markets regardless of which sovereign holds the territory
American merchants resident in New Orleans who have no representation in the Cabildo but whose economic weight is growing and who demand a voice in the city's governance

Free persons of colour, a substantial and economically significant community in New Orleans, whose legal status under Spanish law is more secure than it would be under American jurisdiction and who watch the political transition with acute anxiety
Spanish officials who regard the Cabildo as an instrument of colonial administration rather than genuine self-governance, and who use it to manage Creole elite opinion on behalf of Madrid
public_legitimacy: Recognised as legitimate by the Creole elite whose members compose it and whose interests it serves; regarded with indifference or hostility by American settlers and with wariness by the free Black community, who understand that its replacement by American territorial government may extinguish legal protections they currently enjoy.
plausible_actions: Send a formal remonstrance to the American Congress protesting the terms of the territorial government established by the Orleans Territory Act of 1804, demanding the rights of American citizens rather than the status of colonial subjects
Negotiate with incoming American territorial officials for the preservation of Spanish civil law—particularly regarding property, inheritance, and slavery—as the legal foundation of the new territory
Attempt to maintain public order and municipal services during the interregnum between Spanish and American authority, asserting institutional continuity as a basis for political survival
Cultivate relationships with sympathetic American officials, particularly those who understand that Creole elite cooperation is essential to stable governance of the territory
Document and register existing property titles under Spanish law with urgency, anticipating that American legal challenges to Creole landholdings will follow the transfer
implausible_actions: Declare New Orleans an independent municipality refusing to recognise either French or American sovereignty
Invite British military protection as an alternative to American or French rule
Abolish the institution of slavery within its jurisdiction as a means of securing the loyalty of the city's enslaved population during the transition
